comment: Hand-authored Act-1 dialogue (takes precedence over the generated Drifella barks in content/drifella-barks.json). Two passes: (1) the opening-house cast (npcId->character is a first pass to refine during sprite work, e.g. Biscuit on the dog sprite); (2) a Signal Town MILADY-LORE pass on reachable path NPCs (166/167/191/1410/163/136/202/138/146/1411/135/137) seeding the antagonist's lore through townsfolk rumor: Little Swag World is the organic source, Milady/Remilia corrupts it into hostile classification infrastructure (provenance gates, the public version, the Malady as visible symptom). See docs/swagbound-eb-act1-arc-mapping.md.

pages: A kid with a laminated smile steps into Bosch's lane before Bosch has finished choosing it.
"Hey. You want in? Membership's free — the read is what costs you. We already have a version of you that joined. You're just here to sign for it."
The kid points like an old injury learned to identify suspects.
"It was you. The record says you beat my buddy. It got here before you did."
"Beat it, before I have to become evidence."
He works the gum in his mouth like a clerk processing something wet and official.
"Spit, spit. You want some? Get your own, claimant. My mouth's the only file in town I still control."
"To tell you the truth — and the truth's been getting expensive — I don't remember saying half of what the town has me saying."
"My public version's a better neighbor than I am. Pays his dues, waves first. I just live here."
"Mifella's got a weapon with a reputation bigger than his hand."
"If you take it from him, maybe the town has to admit the source can still beat the brochure."
"You equip what you buy, or it's just a receipt that a better you knew the move."
"There. Handed my own enemy a hint. Confidence, or bad filing. We'll see which survives."
"Evening. Don't worry, you're the version I expected."
"What do you need filed?"
"In, in. Wipe whatever followed you off the mat."
"What can I do for you, the real one?"
Cash matters. I understand. But call your mother before the town does.
Worry's cheap. Calling back is the part nobody can copy yet.
The dog sighs like a manual nobody admits to reading.
Watch the shop window. Glow means upgrade, black means no fit.
Even a dog can tell when a system is ranking the bodies that walk in.
The machine waits the way machines wait — patient, incurious, certain you'll comply.
"Insert your card. We'll take it from here. We always do."
The receiver's still warm. Somebody rehearsed your half of this.
Line's open. Say something before the record beats you to it.
She tilts her head at the door like it owes her money.
Who knocks at this hour? Be a dear and answer. I've decided it isn't for me.
That noise woke the whole house and you're treating fear as optional.
You'll sneak out regardless. Change clothes first, before the street files what you wore.
The dog cracks one eye from the civic sleep all decent animals keep.
People sleep. Dogs sleep. Rumors sleep. Why's your route still awake?
The kid grabs your sleeve like the sentence needed a hostage.
Took my brother out where the rock fell, then the cops bailed for the Sharks.
He's gone. My fault? No. Help me find him before my dad gets home? Yes No
The dog has filed a grievance with the sky and is waiting on a case number.
"(Fleas. Drinking me dry. I'm going to lie here and let them itemize me.)"
"I do what I can, which is mostly hold things and mean well."
"Take it. Roads make a person hungry, and hungry people sign the bad deals."
"Leave me whatever you don't want to carry. I keep it safer than secrets. Hold something? Yes No"
A voice arrives at the door before the face does.
"Somebody's knocking. That rhythm, this hour." A pause that files you. "A knock that wants something."
"Hey. The sound woke you too?"
"Was scared till I remembered: the town scares itself first, then asks us to confirm it happened."
"Feels like I'm always right here."
"It's not loyalty. The place keeps me in the same frame because the frame tests well."
"Welcome, how can I-" He stops. Recognition lands like a collector. "...Wait. You're the guy."
"After we threw down I got serious. Started showing up. Started training."
"Bit of pro wrasslin' now. Turns out losing once is just early-stage rebrand, baby."
"Hold on, kid. I sell hints because somebody monetized not-knowing before we got here."
"Come back when confidence costs more than cash. I'm always open. That's the whole model."
Hello. May I help you, or the other one of you?
I could warn you about the thing in the soup. But warnings work better if I bore you first.
Talk to everyone. The town tells a different story depending on when it thinks you're listening.
Cottage cheese on bread. The latest craze.
A food becomes a movement the second enough mouths agree they found it first.
Lucky sandwiches everywhere now. Fortune cookies, but the prophecy has mayonnaise.
Some give back what you spent. I don't know what you spent. I just know when a snack sounds like a prayer.
Sorry my kids gave you grief. They'll get theirs.
We loaned your dad a hundred grand. Or less. Nobody checks the number anymore.
Now we live in the poverty that loan bought. Aya - DUNG BEETLE. Hold still.
She watches her husband like a screen door in a hurricane.
Soft-hearted. Lets everyone in, signs whatever they hand him. Sweet, until you read what he let through.
He goes down harder than he planned. The surprise is the part that hurts.
I was thinner than my own legend. Weaker than the version of me that talks.
So it starts with you now. Lean close. I've got last words, and these ones are free.
"The kid next door chased a siren and told me to watch the house."
"So I'm watching it. Nothing's happened. That's the watching working."
"His folks are out. The kind of restaurant where the absence shows up on the bill."
"You're the one from next door. They tell me your family's underwater."
"Somebody around here loves itemizing a debt down to the penny."
"Keep plugging. The town forgives slow. But it does file the forgiveness."
"My dad got after me. No dessert. The rest of the decade."
"He said it the way a man reads a verdict he wrote himself."
"I've done my looking at that crater. Looked once. That was the quota."
"The town filed a feeling about it. I'd rather not refile."
"Some things you see once and spend the rest of your life unseeing."
"One night here and the whole party gets re-synced. Anyone who fainted, restored."
"Runs you $100. Sleep's the only clean transaction left."
"Morning, champ. Everyone's topped off. Door filed you out that way."
"Rate's steep. Clean sheets, locked door, a phone that tells the town you survived."
"People pay extra when the confusion's outside, counting heads."
The receiver clicks, the small sound of an institution recognizing a hand.
"Bosch gets to be a voice in another place before he's a body there."
He clocks Bosch the way an actuary clocks a man on a ladder.
"You've got a face the Sharks like to itemize. Walk careful. The town keeps a version of you that already got jumped, and it'd hate to be proven right."
A man dabs his collar, filing a grievance no one will read.
"A pack of kids spat gum on me. On me."
"Now I'm evidence of something I never signed."
She sizes you up like an unscheduled audit.
"There's a process. There's a line."
"You're not even holding a number. Come back when you are."
He watches you with the patience of a camera already running.
"Seen you around. Lots, lately. Heh."
"Don't worry. I'm keeping notes for both of us."
"Town's confused. Confused crowds start auditioning for disaster."
"So we stay cautious. Caution's just surveillance that wears a badge."
"I'd rather be writing parking tickets. A meter doesn't argue about who it is."
"Lately every pedestrian arrives with a public version and a complaint from the future."
He looks too steamed to help, too official to ignore.
"Don't talk to me. I'm busy being angry in a way the file accepts."
He plants himself like a sign that grew a temper.
"So here you are. The one who wandered back down off the high road."
"DO NOT ENTER isn't a wall with words on it."
He stands at the angle of a man waiting for the worst to be official.
"Confusion's just a riot that hasn't finished the instructions yet."
"So we wait. Ultra cautious. To learn which version of today this is."
A gang kid leans in below the soundtrack.
"Don't file me with them. I'm a cop. The costume lies, I don't."
"Badge is real. Rest is wardrobe. Keep it off the record."
"Small fry. You're shaking. The form says suspects shake."
"A hero needs an origin. We just need a report."
"Your average cop beats any hero."
"A hero proves himself in public. We get issued the proof."
"Don't let the Mayor's praise swell your head."
"Civic praise is a leash with nicer words. Fight me before it tightens."
"Right here, right now. The script says that before the kid wins."
"If I lose, I call the boss. There's always another office behind this one."
"Let's go, go og. No, go. Even command slips when the record's nervous."
"Sorry. I meant to sound ready."
MiFella lets himself in like he owns the version of you that counts.
"Bosch! Wake up. You're trending before breakfast. Congrats, I think?"
"There's already a you outside. Cleaner haircut, smoother story."
"He signed a 4am mint: 'Swag is eternal.' The town's already calling him convenient."
"Relax, it's free press. Come let it count. ...You're gonna make a face, aren't you. They love the face."
He checks City Hall like a door he's sure he locked.
"I work for the place. The Mayor sits in my chest wrong. Smiles in the minutes, never in the room."
"Keep that off the record. You and me only."
The clerk reads aloud in the flat warmth of a form letter.
"No openings. No part-time. No nothing. 'At this time' so the no looks like it expires."
"It never expires. We just refile it under a newer date."
He brightens, then catches himself a beat too late.
"Saw something huge up on the high step once. Right where you're headed. And, oh."
"Forget I said it. They don't pay me to leak the route."
A man with a clipboard narrows his eyes toward the trees.
"Suspicious kids in the woods by the library. A gang's just a club the town forgot to stamp."
"So I started a movement. Sweeps the bad influences right out. Hum de dum dum."
"Foul mood. The Mayor doesn't care for me."
"This form could take hours to sign. Stalling a record is the only power this desk hands out."
"You're Bosch. Or a Bosch. Homework done? Teeth brushed? Bed dry?"
"Sorry. Grown-ups turn into checklists when they can't keep a kid off the route."
"Mayor Pirkle. You handled the toughs, which lets peace take the credit after."
"A key to the shack? Take it. But if the record turns violent, this office never signed off."
"Next vote, back Mayor Pirkle."
"A speech sounds sincere when someone else writes the need before you write the words."
"Thank you for your help."
"That's the line for the record. Off the record: thanks for making my file less embarrassing."
"City Hall sits east. Where the sun lands first and the paperwork warms up before anyone."
"Follow the light, if you trust light to tell you what it already touched."
"Trouble makes a market for hints. I'm a small vendor in a big market."
"I'll sell you a direction. I can't sell you the part where the direction sells you back."
"The old digger finally turned up something worth turning up."
"Says he'll only show it to one name. So the treasure already knows who to ask for."
"I wonder if EarthBound has been released yet."
"Sometimes a town waits for its own source material to arrive and explain why everyone sounds borrowed."
A mouse mother holds up her son like a small living exit clause.
"Take him if you get lost. Mazes underestimate anything they can't file as a person."
The mouse waits at the dark road's edge and asks for faith with no paperwork attached.
"Trust me here. Some routes only show up after the record stops watching."
"O o o o. Do I come too?"
"I'm small, I'm quiet, and I'm very hard for any system to explain."
I live rent-free with the Exit mice. They run faster than the paperwork.
When a maze claims you, it files the claim as a clerical error. Then keeps you.
Read a pamphlet: every hotel, every drugstore, a phone. Green costs a dollar. Black is free.
Convenience is surveillance with the instructions printed nice enough to feel like a gift.
"Lately I sense UFOs overhead. Maybe I'm dreaming."
"Dreams are cheaper to dismiss than witnesses, so the town files most witnesses under dreams."
The book says modern life demands a cash machine in every lobby. Demands it.
Took a whole book to make me notice the machines already knew when I was broke.
The person says nothing. In Signal Town, silence still gets transcribed.
He won't talk. He's watching the version of you that showed up early.
He's decided this one waved better. .....
Cramming for the test. Hotels refill everything. A nap fixes the body, not the head.
A quiz about the rules is still the rules quizzing you. Memorize them first.
Studying the Step. North, then west of town. They say it carries a charge.
A place gets powerful when everyone points and nobody asks who collects.
A butterfly went past. Not in any ledger I keep. Just there, being light about it.
Stand near one and your cares unfile themselves. For a second nothing takes attendance.
Who're you visiting? Wheeled one in unconscious. Body's here, tenant's out.
Pay the fee, we hold the body on file till the person comes back to claim it.
Don't laugh. I'm a healer. Licensed by nobody, trusted by the desperate.
Hospital fixes the wounds with names. I take the ones too embarrassing to name.
Turned to stone, turned to mushroom. Conditions they can spell but won't forgive.
We sit in lobbies because the shame always checks in before the patient does.
I'll examine each of you. Small fee. Cheaper than a funeral.
The body turns into a line item, and the line item proves somebody's still in there.
Round here they say our doctor's number one.
That's what the lobby says. Lobbies make waiting sound like a choice.
Intake printer blinks: ROUTE FATIGUE ACCEPTED.
Clean breath. I can reset your meters. Not your story. Nobody resets that.
"A mouse put me here. A small mouse."
"The little ones hit hardest. The record never logs them, so they swing for free."
"Here for my daughter? You're the one who calls at night and talks for hours."
"Then why'd your version come down the line an hour before you came through the door?"
"Came to visit a friend. Slipped in the hall. Now I'm a patient too."
"That's the sad telling. The civic telling files it under efficient intake."
He says it flat, which is its own kind of dare.
"Doing this because I trust you. Basement first. We're not even kin."
"You've got me filed as a liar. Fair. But records get revised. Maybe I surprise us both."
"You've grown. Starting to look like a man."
"Or the town's idea of one. I prefer the rougher cut. The one still deciding."
"Dreamed you were traveling with a pretty girl."
"If the dream gets filed as record, tell her I said hi. Single folk flirt with futures."
"Take my cap. You've eyed it long enough."
"Wear something with a history. The route hates an object that remembers whose hands it passed through."
The kid shakes Bosch awake like an alarm clock he doesn't respect.
"You're up. Mifella's crew scattered, left me holding the dark. Typical."
"Home. Before they file us both as missing. Funny -- I'm older, you're the one they copy."
He stands very still, like that counts as doing something.
"Heard a voice up the hill. Didn't check. I'm a busy man. When I work, I work clean."
The hill keeps the voice. He keeps the excuse. Everyone's holding something.
He guards the sign like the sign guards a secret. It does not.
"Board says Do Not Enter. You read, or do I read it to you?"
Go in anyway? Yes No
He's watched the hideout so long his eyes file their own reports.
"Lookout duty. Done my shift twice over. Take it, would you?"
Yes No
City Hall took the key the way a clerk takes a name -- quietly, for the file.
"Mifella's crew trashed the place, so it's sealed. Can't even change clothes."
He says Mifella like it's already on a list, and he's just reading it back.
"A library's honest work. Ignorance comes in, leaves with a due date."
"Got every Swag World fact in here. Maps too -- borrow free."
"Only loan in town that doesn't grow teeth."
Something surfaces from the dirt, having read your file and found it thin.
"Easy. Just an average mole. I have opinions about violence."
Want a few hints on fighting? Yes No
Past the gate the air starts itemizing you. Little ticks, like a register that can see.
That's the threshold. That's where Milady stops being a rumor and becomes a form you have to sign.
I walked up the line once to ask Milady a question.
The line asked me three back, kept the answers, and mailed me a cleaner version of the question. Never went again.
There's a point overlooking the sea, off thataway. Best view in the district.
Some horizons don't argue. They just take the file out of your hands.
Gets me in the throat every time. Don't log that I said so.
Everyone north of here works for Milady. Nobody north of here will say so.
That's the benefit package. You stop being able to say it, and in exchange you stop having to.
Everything here points up the line. Up the line is the yard, and the yard is Milady's.
And Milady's is everywhere, which is exactly how you know it's nowhere good.
Seen your public version yet? Smiles better than you. Answers before you do.
Milady prefers it, honestly. Less upkeep, no opinions of its own.
I'm not scared of Milady.
I'm scared of how reasonable she makes the paperwork sound. You sign before you notice you agreed.
They say Milady was born down in the source somewhere. They say she's already pregnant with the next one.
They say a lot. The saying sticks better than the truth. That stickiness IS the machine.
He smiles like a contract that signed itself and is waiting for you to notice.
This house? $7,500. Ocean view. The one address the town can't lock or copy.
A deed is the only version of you nobody can route around. Buy it?
Yes No
Hey. You've got your own place now. A real one, not a route.
Small fact that outlived a big town. Rarest kind there is.
He cradles the trumpet like the one possession the records haven't claimed yet.
"Happiest man alive. Scenic spot, good acoustics, nobody to tell me no."
"I'll send a haunting little melody clean across town. Let the public version of me try to play it back."
He squares up before you've decided anything, hungry to be your bad day.
You want to start something, or what?
He grins like a doorman working the rope outside a beating.
You want to meet Mifella? Course you do. You don't mind your name rearranged.
Real you or route you, the card clique files everybody the same.
Milady? Yeah, she's like, the brand. It's on everything down here. I stopped reading it.
Probably fine. It's usually fine. Why, did you read it?
My feed says Milady loves us. My feed also says I won a boat.
I did not win a boat. But I keep checking, you know? Just in case the boat's real and the love isn't.
That Malady up the route? That's only the part you're allowed to see.
Milady's the part already inside your distribution. You can't out-run your own reach.
The door's locked. The town is very good at that part.
Guardian checks the latch, then checks Bosch's pulse. Both, in that order.
Rest here. The house holds your place while the route tries to edit it.
A man leans uphill, already rehearsing the version of himself that arrived first.
"Something fell up there. First eyes own the story. I have owned nothing. I'd like a turn."
"Try and beat me. The copy of you already left."
He talks in fragments, like a confession the footnotes keep interrupting.
"Don't panic! It's just a... what... a rock that fell. I just want... who... to go home."
"The road's shut. They close it the way a clerk shuts a window: right as you reach it."
"We're going for the record anyway. The record doesn't care about the road. Neither do we."
She stares down the closed road like it filed paperwork against her personally.
"I want to go home. The road says no. One rock falls and the whole town acts processed."
The voice arrives before the face does, all curfew, no warmth.
"Don't you know what time it is? Get home. Before something logs that you didn't."
He recites his grievances in order, like reading items off a long receipt.
"A rock landed. The card gang's loose. You're sightseeing. And I'm hungry."
"I hate this job. Put that in the record too."
"The hilltop's dangerous. I'm advising against it, which is paperwork admitting it won't work."
"You'll go anyway. They always do. Sign here so the blame routes elsewhere."
A man surfaces from the dark, patient as a closing window.
"You. Curfew's a service we render. You're past your appointment."
The cop runs the same line on a loop, a stamp pressed until the ink gives out.
"Clear out. Get out of my way. Clear out. Get out of my way. Clear out. Of way out get my —"
"Whoops. My mistake. I mean: get out of my way."
"Shh. They're recording the quiet, and you're not in it yet."
She catches Bosch's eye like a witness who's relieved someone finally showed up to take a statement.
"Good timing. Will you do something about Pokey? He's a one-man membership drive and I'm exhausted."
"Are you two friends?"
Yes    No
The crater-light has him hooked, lips moving on a word with no end.
"That rock. It doesn't match anything on file. Marvelous. Unstamped. o o o o o!"
He waves you back, half rule, half mercy.
"Stop gawking. You're in the officer's frame. He's logging it, and you're not cleared to appear."
"Big footprint up the hill behind the shack, they say."
"Never saw it. The town agrees it's there without checking. A rumor with perfect attendance."
Remilia's the one holding the stamp.
Milady's the one who decided there'd BE a stamp. Different job. Same smirk.
Milady doesn't run Signal Town. The town runs ON her, like a charge nobody itemized.
You'll meet her paperwork long before you meet her. Most people stop at the paperwork.
I got FREAKING FIRED FROM MILADY'S for asking what the job actually was.
Turned out the job was being me, at scale, badly. They had a cleaner me by lunch.
DO NOT ENTER, bolted where a welcome should be.
The font has won every argument it's ever had.
Used to be Little Swag World was just where we were from. No stamp, no fee.
Then Milady filed it as a 'source' and started charging us rent on ourselves. She calls that provenance.
The plaque swears this is the right house.
Bosch files it like anything from a man whose name is a confession spelled sideways.
Heat comes off the wreckage in slow waves, like it's deciding what to keep.
"Fire's still working. Burns here, then there. Like it's auditing what's left to take."
Don't stare at the cards too long. The cards read you back.
Whatever they find, they file straight up the line. Everything up the line is Milady's.
It stands in the one spot the map calls sacred, and grins like ownership is a thing you can grin into being.
"You finally got here. First Sanctuary on the list. Used to be yours, the brochure says. It's mine now. Come collect, if the source has the nerve."
Welcome. We log the order, we log the face. Mostly the order.
What'll it be?
You look hungry, or you look like the last guy who looked hungry.
Same difference. What do you need?
Step up. The counter doesn't bite. It files, but it doesn't bite.
Help you with something?
Two of you came in today. The other one didn't tip.
So. How can I help?
Expand the mind, mon. The merchandise expands the wallet.
Come on. Buy some of my beautiful junk.
I sell off the street, off the books, off the conscience.
The flies are seasoning. They volunteered.
Bananas? Good for the potassium. Bad for nothing else.
I move a lot of them. Don't ask where the peels go.
Seasonings? Make every meal taste like it remembers a happier version of itself.
Miraculous. Nobody asks where the flavor's been filed.
The statue glows wrong. A light with paperwork.
Bosch watches it the way you watch a door opening on its own.
"I've been waiting. Help me make the world blue. Simple. Easy to process."
"Be my right hand. A hand signs faster once the head stops asking who wrote the form."
"Aai i i i e e e e!"
"I screamed because nobody had issued me a better response yet."
"Blue, blue. Want to be one of us? Stop what you're doing and meet Mr. Carpainter."
"He loves a claimant who arrives pre-flattened."
"Blue, blue. Am I in your way?"
"I'll move. Even devotion yields to a route with a stronger appointment."
"Blue, blue. Don't bother us. We're praying."
"Fine. I'll move. The line to heaven is still a line."
"Blue, blue. You think I can only walk in place?"
"Watch. Even a loop can perform dissent if someone files the footage wrong."
"Green, green. No. That's last week's color. I haven't updated."
"I'm new here. My mouth hasn't been issued the current palette yet."
"Blue, blue. I'll stand wherever the room needs me to believe."
"Faith folds flat. Easy to carry when they rearrange the furniture."
"Blew, blew. I'm practicing my whistle. Whistlers smile."
"Train the mouth happy and maybe nobody audits the eyes."
"Blue, blue. Full speed ahead. Or whatever the official phrasing was."
"Now I crush you. Violence goes down smoother once it borrows a procedure."
"Blue, blue. Don't disrupt the meeting."
"A meeting is just a mob that kept minutes. Minutes can vote to stomp you."
"Blue, blue. Strange, unmasked fellow."
"Don't go up there. The good place has an intake desk, and your face is still arguing with itself."
"Blue, blue. Anti-blue boy."
"You bother me. You keep showing up in a color the doctrine can't compress."
"Blue, blue. Non-believers are just unfiled. We fix that."
"A bad category is anything the village hasn't gotten around to forgiving yet."
"Blue, blue. This'll wake you right up."
"Forced awakening looks a lot like getting knocked cold. Same paperwork either way."
"Blue, blue. Sharp fit. Suspiciously sharp."
"Fabric confesses before the wearer does. The doctrine loves an easy witness."
"Blue, blue. Why's an un-blue thing standing here?"
"The question's a fence. Short on purpose. Proud of it, too."
"Blue, blue. No happiness without the membership."
"Joy runs smoother once it's a requirement and somebody's holding the roster."
The man counts the empty chairs like they owe him.
"Most everyone woke up and walked. Roster thinned overnight, no reason mailed."
He's half-turned for the door, coat already deciding for him.
"Three of us left. I won't be the last name on a list nobody reads. Gone."
Whatever was holding him upright just let go. Underneath: tired.
"Sorry. For all of it. No dues, no version of me you forgive twice."
"When the world goes blue, the country runs quiet. You follow?"
"Agreement is sweeter as comprehension. Doubt we file under confusion."
"Hear me wrong if you must. The boss's voice rewires a room."
"A warning's no good when it has to whisper inside the thing it warns you of."
"We came to join the blue. Married. From out past the bridge."
"Two people make one file, if they stand close and answer blue together."
Two travelers stand too close, the way same-file people do.
"Our kid's downtown, waiting on his record to clear."
"Wanted to bring him something real before the stand prints an easier one."
"This is the counseling office. Get happy, then report to headquarters."
"Counseling preps the person. Headquarters preps the version worth keeping."
"I not know you. My kind live in the valley, over there."
"Bye bye. Outsiders bring papers first, questions second."
Hello. You here for help, or just here to be on record?
Either way, I've got a form for it.
"The refreshing herb says it helps colds, poison, depression, and the illnesses that won't heal cleanly."
"An herb with copy on the back is still a plant trying to survive marketing."
You lift the receiver. The silence on the line is already shaped like you.
"Someone's waiting to hear if your voice matches the one we filed."
"It usually does. That's the worrying part."
We're one big happy family. Officially, only family stays.
Learn to smile our way and maybe you rent a bed.
Refuse, and kinship stops being a feeling and starts being a policy.
Blue... blue. I'll examine each of you. For a fee.
The body has to tint itself the right color before we'll agree it's treatable.
Blue... blue. Who'd you come to see? The patient's unconscious.
Pay the doctor and we'll keep the file breathing until the person catches up.
The kid wears his importance like a borrowed coat two sizes too generous.
"You're here to bug me. Call me Master Pokey — Carpainter stamped me important, made it official in Happy-Happyism. You should join. You won't."
"I'm glad I signed. I won't fight you myself — that's what these guys are filed under. Later, potater."
He glares at the dead bridge like it broke a promise to him personally.
"Who closes a crossing? A perfectly good way across, just... gone. Crud."
A man hums at the dirt, hunting for caps, eyes down.
"Hum dee dum. Mushrooms, mushrooms. Say. That one on your head."
"Fifty bucks, it's mine. No questions, no paperwork."
"I said the word once and the sky took it. Some words are one-spend only."
"Fixed the bridge to square the ledger. I'm useful now. On the record, anyway."
A mole surfaces, blinking, harmless as a clerk at quitting time.
"Not an enemy. Just a mole filing overtime. Want the rules nobody hands you till you've lost? Yes   No"
Cross the bridge. The next town files its happiness in advance.
A pencil-shaped iron statue blocks the path, older than the path.
Nobody filed for it. Nobody moves it. It's the town's opinion, cast in iron.
He's already standing where the second sanctuary used to belong to someone else.
"You made it. The place is mine now. Possession beat paperwork, like always."
"Take it back, if your version of the story outlasts mine."
"You looking to buy, or just confirming you exist? Both cost the same."
"Step up. Everything's priced. Even the looking, if you do it long enough."
Hello. May I help you, or the version of you that came in first?
Ssshhh. Voices get filed in here.
State your business. Quietly.
I dreamt we took a walk. Slow. No one watching.
Then you showed up troubled, like the dream filed its report ahead of you.
No snack. Stuck on my project, missing the teacher who explained the rules.
Take this bent key for the lockers. Nothing stays straight after passing through here.
It holds like bad news holds. Completely. No apology.
Locked.
Heading out? Say hi to Maxwell. He keeps an eye on the small ones.
Dangerous animals roam the grounds. The campus files it under extracurricular.
So sleepy. Tony's birthday is tomorrow.
I iced every cookie by hand. Proof each was loved before the party made it public.
Welcome in. We file every customer twice now, just to be safe.
First copy's free. The second one already shopped here this morning.
Monkey by the door. Buy gum and he's yours, otherwise a dollar.
He signs no paperwork. Smart. Cheaper to be a monkey than a citizen.
The gum here is unreal. Blow a bubble and watch.
For one second the world's got a soft spot the record can't reach into.
The monkey gives one answer and refuses every form around it.
"Kyee kyee. Yeah."
Three flat beeps. No hello, no name behind it.
Some lines would rather hang up than admit they know you.
I cook for the lake-watching crowd. Waiting goes down easier with stew.
Gives the newspaper something cozy to crop out before they run the photo.
"If I find Tessie, do I get in the newspaper?"
"Fifteen minutes of fame is just a public version with a shorter lease."
The second phone runs the same dead little alarm.
A line can be empty and still keep the dial tone busy.
The man starts a sales pitch, then the pitch slides off his face entirely.
"Cave guy sent you, sure, sure -- wait. No. That walk. That's my kid's walk."
"Ten years, and the record skipped every one. You look filed-and-found."
"Sit. Have a donut before I weep into the till. Yes / No"
He hands over the machine like an alibi he can't afford to keep.
"They took the inventor mid-sentence. He was on the line calling you. Then just -- erased."
"I stood here and did nothing. So take it. Let the machine do the part I couldn't."
Two newlyweds, talking past you in a dialect the town never bothered to file.
"Kekye koo kikkro. (Thank you for minding my husband. And -- we finally made it official.)"
"Kooki kekye ki! (Quit gaping like an unsigned form, dear, say hello!)"
The husband finds his words the way newlyweds find each other's debts.
"Krikko kyakya. (That cave north of the old stones -- you wondered what's filed inside. Admit it.)"
"Keke kike. (Go look. We're off -- still newlyweds, you understand. Bye-bye.)"
(Beep... beep... beep...)
(Something in the room keeps time for a meeting nobody scheduled.)
Instant Revitalizing Device.
(Step in, step out rested -- a full night's sleep compressed into a receipt you never earned.)
"There you are. Taller. Older. No. No time for that."
"Tony's gone. I figured he was filed under you."
"A responsible boy leaves a paper trail. This one left silence."
"Use me. Climb. Over the gate you go."
"Your road's your business. I'm not asking."
"Just keep one record that never expires: best friends, forever."
"Kyi kya kiko kree. (I saw you once. You know my fiance.)"
"He went to chew gum and see the doctor. Never came back."
"Now I can't cross the lake. I picture that doctor signing him up for a crash test."
"The call came through. Gentler than the silence before it."
"He's with the doctor now. They might invent something true."
"You did this quietly, off the books. A decent life, no name on the receipt."
"He doesn't call. Maybe I'm too much. You didn't tell him to stop... no."
"No. You wouldn't."
"If you see him out there, tell him to call Tony. The line stays open."
"You finally showed. This sanctuary's logged under me now."
"Possession, nine-tenths, all that. Take it back if your nerve clears the paperwork."
"You finally got here. Third sacred spot. Mine now."
"Take it if you dare. Sacred only lasts until someone files possession first."
Long road behind you. I can smell the mileage.
Don't explain. Hunger keeps better records than you do.
Hi hi hi. You buying, or just casing the shelves?
You can sell, too. Everything here came in someone's pocket.
Hi hi hi. Buying? The good stock walked in on legs.
You can sell, too. We take anything that has a past.
Hello there. How may I process you today?
We run an inn out back. One night, cheap as a rumor.
No? Wise. The beds are dusty, and dust remembers travelers better than we do.
The receiver clicks like a room pretending not to listen.
Your voice arrives first. The rest of you is always late to the call.
Small house. Stay the night anyway.
While you sleep I'll fix what's broken. Repair returns a thing to itself before morning files it as trash.
The receiver is already awake.
"Every call asks the same question: are you the source, or just the voice that arrived fastest?"
Keep it down. The walls take notes, and they're behind on filing.
State your business. Quietly.
Welcome. Everything's priced. Even the things that aren't for sale.
Step in. Browse slow. The shelves remember who lingers.
Evening. You're the second of you through that door today. The first didn't buy.
Seasonings. I sell the flavor; the regret's on the house.
What's it going to be?
Yes? Point at a thing and I'll tell you what it costs to want it.
Lower your voice. Loud customers get logged twice.
What do you need, and how fast can you need it elsewhere?
Door's open. The goods are real.
Realer than most things this side of the corner, anyway.
Headed down the coast? Stock up here first.
Prices down there bite. Up here they only nibble.
Welcome. Everything's priced, nothing's free.
Tell me what you're short on.
No grown-ups with you? Bold.
Kids wandering in alone. Somebody filed that wrong.
She studies the kids like a typo in her own brochure.
"Serious faces in a town that sells the opposite."
"People come here to forget there's a record at all."
"I'm a surfer. Officially. The sea declined to confirm."
"All rumor, no waves."
"Maybe I go porter. Haul bags, collect on a debt the ocean forgot."
"Sky's blue, sun's loud, prices in a hurry."
"Pay enough and your face goes blue to match."
"The view's free. Standing in it costs."
"Bored? Go read the walls at the museum. They keep a glyph somebody pried out of a tomb."
"Old writing's still writing. The dead leave cleaner directions than the living."
"Another clean day. This town hands you sun like it never learned any other trick."
(A Myna bird. It watches Bosch the way a witness watches a doorway, certain it'll be asked to repeat something later.)
"Want the far shore? You cross open sea. And the sea keeps a tenant. Answers to no harbor."
"He works the ships that pass. Calls it a toll. The water signed nothing. He collects anyway."
"Scared? Smart men are. Cross it, or stay home and be a story someone tells."
He grabs your sleeve like a man whose only evidence already rotted.
"A monster, real, I saw it. Surfaced when the ground dried, then quit being alive about it."
"Dead now. But I saw it. Believe me, or join the people who filed the truth as inconvenient."
"Boat ride? I run the only honest crossing left. Yes, or no."

Yes
No
Fsst. Fsst. The sound of something deciding whether you count as a problem yet.
Meow. Flat, official, like a clerk stamping a form it has no intention of reading.
The baker waits behind a cake that looks too pleased with itself.
"You came a long way for my cake. So somebody filed your route before you walked it."
"Dig in. It's all leftovers, reassembled into something special. Like most of us."
He stands too still, the way a man stands when he's being mistaken for furniture.
"East of here is the port town. That's the one true thing I know."
"...No. I'm not signage. Stop reading me like a sign."
He stands too still, the way a man stands when he's being mistaken for furniture.
"East of here is the port town. That's the one true thing I know."
"...No. I'm not signage. Stop reading me like a sign."
He scans for fire, finds none, and lets his face drop back to factory default.
"Nothing's wrong here. Nothing I've been told to report."
He wheels on Bosch like a car alarm that just found a reason to live.
"Hey! Quit smearing your fingerprints on my car, punk."
"That's evidence I didn't authorize."
The hotel. Name's fancier than the carpet.
"A room's a hundred fifty a night. Buys a door, a bed, and a receipt."
"The receipt remembers it cheaper than you will."
"We serve water, gruel, and whatever else won't generate complaints."
"Welcome, welcome! Ach, this heat. Is the body still cooperating?"
"Buy something. The merchandise won't sell itself, though it has tried."
Ssshh. Walls file what they hear.
State your business and keep it short.
Hsss. Red Snake. Door's open, count your fingers.
I trade in heat and bad ideas. Step closer.
If you already carry what you need, walk on.
If you don't, that's a shame I can sell you.
I sell water to the thirsty. The trick is making them thirsty first.
Seasonings? A little dust to make the day taste filed.
Sprinkle it. Forget the recipe. Miraculous.
Welcome to my little stall, far from any registry.
Try the local delicacies. Nobody's logged them yet.
The dead sub still hums, pretending to be alive.
"Yes. It can still work," the hull admits, like old machines do.
A Good Night Bench. Promises to fix you, same as every other thing in town.
Sit. Recover health and spirits. No membership. Just stay long enough to mean it.
Bosch lifts the receiver. The line was already open, waiting on him.
Another Good Night Bench, worn smooth by everyone who needed it first.
Sit. Let your health come back from wherever it wandered. No charge. No record kept.
This bench was built with intent. More than most furniture can claim.
Check in here like a hotel. Cheaper. Honester. No clerk reads your name back.
- Brick Road
A third Good Night Bench, patient as a clerk who read your file and stayed kind.
Sit. Recover health and spirits. This offer never expires, which makes it rare here.
It doesn't rise so much as assume it was always standing there, owed.
"You finally made it. Seventh Sanctuary on the list, the read says. It was yours on paper."
"It's mine now. Come take it back, claimant. If your hands are better than your paperwork."
It softens, briefly, the way even hard things do when nobody's watching the ledger.
"Thanks for talking to me. People walk past most of what's down here without ever signing for it."
The rock has the air of something holding one thought for a geological while.
The talking rock down here wants to talk. Been waiting longer than the records have.
The rock sits where the cave folds in on itself, smug as a notarized deed.
This is the earth's belly button. The one spot that remembers being attached to something.
He's found a fascinating patch of floor and intends to study it forever.
"We're shy. It's the only credential we've kept current."
The words arrive out of order, like a form filled out from a description.
"In the hole, inn we have. Free. Stay?"
Free lodging in the belly of the world. You pay in conversations you'll never finish.
"We're all shy. Down here it's the one fact nobody disputes."
"Rumor says there's a book that cures it. A whole book about being looked at."
"Where? Nobody's brave enough to go ask. That, more or less, is the trouble."
This one turns a quarter away, offering Bosch a shoulder and a wish to be elsewhere.
Two stand close, drawing courage from proximity. The supply runs short.
"We're shy." The pause does the rest of the talking.
He inspects his own hands, hoping they'll volunteer an excuse to leave.
He opens his mouth, reconsiders, closes it. The speech stays behind his teeth.
It holds very still. If it doesn't move, maybe nothing files it.
The smallest one gives up the one fact it trusts.
"..... Shy... that we are..."
Says it like a password to a club nobody wanted in.
"Evening. Talking rock. Don't act shocked, it offends the geology."
"My cousins keep quiet. The real chatterbox is deep down the dark below."
"Go talk to it. The record checks you on that one later."
"One of us isn't shy. Me. We capped the hole 'cause something old lives under us."
"Went down once. Came right back up, on account of being so surprised."
"A stone down there talks and talks. The quiet one's strong, just shy. Don't we all."
Bosch lifts the receiver. A line that had been waiting for him to admit he was findable opens up.
A sign by the steaming water. No hand around that painted it.
"Hot springs. Good all over. The kind of clean the town can't revoke once you've had it."
The little thing bounces when it talks, punctuation that lost its sentence.
"Kidnapped, I was. Boing! Then the bad guy went gone. Zoom!"
"Where to? Backward. Everybody gets filed somewhere older than here."
He starts twice, stops twice, reading bad news off his own face.
"This is hard to say. I'll say it slow, the way town says things it can't take back."
"Giygas attacks from the past. To reach him you warp there — through the Phase Distorter 3. But the machine won't carry the living. Flesh dies in the crossing. That's not cruelty. That's the spec."
"So we move only the program. Your spirit goes into a robot and the robot goes back. Your body stays here, signed out and waiting, while the better, quieter version of you does the fighting."
"I can't promise the spirit returns. The four of you are the chosen ones — which is the town's polite word for the ones who don't get to decline. Still go? Yes  No"
He won't meet your eyes, the way clerks won't when the form ends badly.
"You have to go back and fight. You have to. The rest I keep losing on purpose."
Two little ones crowd in, bouncing before anyone agreed to anything.
"We come too! Boing! Wherever the record goes, we get listed under it."
Something small sits in the wreckage where the future used to idle.
An item left behind, like a receipt the machine couldn't finish printing.

INPUT YOUR COMMAND.
  Life-up    Communicate
Buy    Sell
He scratches the spot where the explanation was supposed to land.
"I heard the whole speech the talking rock gave. Heard every word."
"Understood none of it. Some files you can hold and never open."
He swings the cage door like a favor he was ordered to do.
"What are you doing in the dinosaur's cage? Out. Boss's orders."
"And thanks for the item. It's been taken off you. So's that smell."
"We're the same tribe as the ones up top. Same stock, same filing."
"Seem different? The ones up there are shy. Down here we've got nobody to perform for."
He says it low, the way men trade rumors that change nothing.
"The folk up above have some beautiful women. Still won't talk."
"Beauty and silence, shelved on the same row."
"You're strange. But you smell good, so here's a secret like it's contraband."
"Feel a quake? After one shakes through, the springs erupt. The only schedule that keeps its promise."
"Blue springs bring health back. Red ones pull the poison up. Pick your wound, pick your color."
He talks fast, like someone whose silence used to lose every argument.
"We came down because the quiet up above outvoted us. So we filed for our own tribe."
"Someday we'll be neighbors again. ...It was easier up there. Most true things are."
A small one squints at Bosch like he's auditing a rumor.
"My brother says we caged the dinosaurs ourselves. I don't believe a word."
"He just runs his mouth till something falls out. None of it matches the file."
The Tenda soaks in the steam, nursing a wound no thread will close.
"Some loud kid said a thing that landed wrong and never left."
"Words don't dissolve. But the hot springs let you pretend they do."
This one extends a hand with the worn confidence of a man who's filled out forms in three languages.
"You're foreign, yes? Good. I am a world-wise Tenda. I studied abroad once, in a country that called itself an economic superpower and believed it."
"Name's Ay-go Stikke. Anything you need processed, claimant, you come to me."
The voice arrives before the body does, patient as a thing that has waited entire civilizations for the appointment.

"You finally came. And you finally talk to me. Good. Take notes if you have to. You're the chosen one. Your destiny isn't yours alone — it belongs to the whole universe, and one day every version of you across it will overlap into one."
"Remember Giant Step in Onett? That was one of Your Sanctuaries. A power spot. A monster guarded it, swollen on the place's own strength, and you put it down."
"There are eight. Reach all eight, let the Sound Stone record every melody, and the last door opens — Your World, finally visible."
The Tenda nods at the bird like a coworker who started speaking in code.
"It keeps saying things. <Hello?> Then <click beep beep beep.>"
"I quit asking what it means. Some transmissions aren't meant for us."
"Ring, ring, rrriiing!!"
The bird holds the note like it's billing someone for the call.
"All this way out to the edge of the map. The road took its cut, I see."
"A bed runs you $ . You're staying. The ones who get this far always do."
A sign, hand-lettered, nailed where the big ones can read it and won't.
"(Do not feed the things in the cage. -- The Boss)"
Another sign, same hand. The kind of rule that appears the instant before you need it.
"(In case of danger, evacuate here. -- The Boss)"
"This shop deals in puddings and pendants. Don't ask which is which."
You'd like to buy some, wouldn't you?
Hi, how's it going? Be honest, the room already filed an answer.
From the look of you, I'd say: under review.
"There's a inside."
Flat. Like a man reading the only line on a blank inventory.
The Malady's already up there past the gate, wearing a face.
Could be anyone's. Could be the better-lit version of yours. That's the part nobody likes to test.
The gate isn't cruel. It just needs you to prove normal, show left, match background.
Milady wrote the form. Nobody has ever passed it clean. That part isn't a bug.
My cousin tried to opt out of provenance.
Now there's a cleaner cousin downtown, doing him better than he ever did.
We don't speak. He files reports.
Something waits in the dark, already catalogued before he showed up.
"There's an inventory in here. You're on it."
"Something's inside." Handed over like a receipt for a thing he never bought.
The Bosch Terminal boots from a timestamp Bosch has not reached yet.
"Bosch, the route is not neutral. A public version is already moving through town ahead of your body."
"That version is the Malady: symptom, mask, and civic convenience with your smile stapled on."
"Milady is the engine underneath it. Rumor, paperwork, feed pressure, all one cheap machine."
"Walk the route anyway. Make the public version prove provenance in daylight."
MiFella hits the door like the algorithm owes him rent.
"Bosch. Up. You're trending before breakfast, which is legally the worst kind of trending."
"Some public version of you is already outside, polite and wrong. Town's eating it up."
"Bring shoes. Bring a face you can prove is yours. The trouble is making good time."
The Public Version Clique doesn't crowd you. It arranges itself around you, smiling, like a search result that already knows your name.
"Relax, Bosch. We did the hard part. There's a cleaner you, signed and ready. Just nod and let this one count."
"Refuse and you stop being a person and start being a dispute. ...Oh, you're going to dispute it. Adorable. Cataloged."
They step into the route like they own the receipt. Whatever you do next, they'll file as the fight starting.
